key_points: The story is about Dr. John Dolittle, a kind doctor who lives in Puddleby-on-the-Marsh.
He loves animals so much that his human patients stop visiting because his house is full of pets.
His parrot, Polynesia, reveals a secret: animals have their own languages and talk using sounds and body parts.
Dr. Dolittle decides to stop being a 'people's doctor' and becomes an 'animal doctor' instead.
He becomes world-famous because he is the only doctor who can actually understand what animals say about their pain.
examples: Polynesia teaches him bird language, starting with simple phrases like 'Ka-ka oi-ee, fee-fee' (Is the porridge hot yet?).
Jip the dog shows him that dogs talk with their noses, ears, and tails (e.g., twitching a nose to ask a question).
A plough horse visits him and explains he isn't sick; he just needs 'green spectacles' (glasses) because he is going blind in one eye.
The 'Cat’s-meat-Man' is the one who first suggests the Doctor should treat animals because he knows more about them than real vets.
Birds fly all over the world to spread the news of the great animal doctor, making him famous in faraway lands.
formulas_or_processes: The Switch → Loves animals → Loses human patients → Learns animal language → Becomes a famous vet.
The Learning Process → Listen to Polynesia → Write 'animal ABCs' in a book → Practice with Jip the dog.
Animal Logic → Humans think animals 'don't complain' just because humans don't understand their language.
The 'Success' Secret → Understanding the patient + Kind treatment = Perfect cure.
exam_tips: Who taught the Doctor? Polynesia the parrot.
Why did he become a vet? Because human patients were scared of his pets and he preferred animals.
What was the horse's problem? He needed glasses (spectacles), not medicine for 'spavins'.
How did animals talk? Not just with mouths, but with tails, feet, and noses.
